speaker: interviewer
text: Her maiden name was Wos. Would you continue telling us about when you were in the barn with the Germans-- with the Russian soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was very bad experience, because I try in-- to run off from this barn. But it was high, too-- too-- too-- high in this barn. And I fall down under this horse. In the meantime, he came with other soldiers, and they were looking for me. They shoot. I heard it close to my head. But I lost conscious laying down under this belly-- big belly of this horse. I don't know what's happened later. In the morning, very early morning, I heard name, [? Orena. ?] [? Orena, ?] this is my nickname, middle name. Then I left this place between these horses. And when my mother saw me, she couldn't believe I was yellow-- completely yellow. She took me with help of other girl, and we left, going only through fields, not on the streets or highways-- only on fields going to west-- to east-- to Poland. When I came on the way, I was in so-- such pain that I-- one day, I saw a couple dead animal. I hide behind them. I wanted to die because of pain.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of pain?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] You know, liver-- liver pain is very, very hard. And I was swollen, yellow, high fever. And each movement, it was-- it was for me, very hard. This pain I felt from up to the down. I wanted to die, but you see, my mother and my sister, they [LAUGHS] found some wagon, like, for children, for dolls. They put me to this, and they took me to Poznan.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the cause of your pain?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, because we-- during this arise from [SIGHS] Spandau, we didn't have source of water. We-- we took water from gutters. And my mother took through the skirt or for something. It was animals, but not-- probably viruses, and bacterias was in. And probably, it was a-- a very, very, very bad infection of liver.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do-- how were you cured?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, I was long time. I was still in a shocked and in depression, but not eating-- like, for liver-- very light food. Still, I can't-- and after that, I start to have stones, because this bile was going to-- to be in stones. And 20 couple years I suffer for liver. Even even now, I have some changes in my liver. Therefore, this is not nice to talk about it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway-- bad experience. What happened when you arrived in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, when-- to Warsaw was very far on Poznan. My mother and my sister went to work in the field to make some money, to give me maybe one roll. I could eat only one small roll, because not dark bread. And then they took me to a railroad station. And men from post train, they saw that I'm very sick. They put me under the-- to this wagon, and we came to Warsaw. And in Warsaw, nobody can recognize even own house. It was ruin, ruin, and ruin. And even entrance to this basement, where it was couple things from our house, was covered with bricks and dust. And you see, it was very difficult. Therefore, first night, we spent in police station. I was laying down on the floor. And one-- and and this day, it was miracle, because this day my friend who was living close to us, [INAUDIBLE]-- I remember, he was fighting in uprising. And he were wounding in couple places in pulmonary.

speaker: interviewee
text: He stand up, and he say, Wanda you are? Yes. You came from-- but he was sick, very sick. He said, don't worry. Don't move.

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought me roll with ham. I was crying to eat this. And later, he took me to his-- my mother. And me, and my sister-- he took me to his small room, and he gave me own bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] When was this when you returned to Warsaw? What time of year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- end of war. It was 9 of May, 1945. I could come maybe in two weeks. I don't remember. Maybe two weeks it took-- took it. But later, we had a summer-- summer house close to Warsaw. And my mother took me to-- with sister-- took me to the summer house. But was so many people running from other to-- place to other, [LAUGHS] looking for somebody. No apartment was open, and we slept in a coal basement for-- for coal and wood, because was everything was taken-- for a couple of days. And later, my mother found some jewelry still. And she paid one family to go-- go some-- to other place. And we had apartment. After that, I went to Gdynia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who returned to that apartment after the war-- in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this summer house? Yes, because I told you that in Warsaw, it was destroyed everything. But in [NON-ENGLISH], it was close to Warsaw. This-- it was summer house when we had vacation or we visited very often. It was big-- big yard. People who were homeless, and they took whatever was. They grabbed. They took. Nobody ask if I can. They took place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who in your family returned to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my mother, my sister, me. But later came from west, from liberation in American zone, by-- my father, my aunt, and my brother. My brother from England, who was fighter in Warsaw, he was in [NON-ENGLISH], a solider. He came later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: George. He came later, still in battle dress. He was hero from uprising. He transport ammunition from center of Warsaw to Old Town. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after the war in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: In after the war, I was one year in-- because was no apartment in Warsaw. And I had very close, this Mischka Biedermann. Her mother took me to Gdynia, where apartment of her family was perfect. And I-- I joined gymnasium in this-- in high school in in Gdynia. I was there for one year. But good or not-- but still, I was rebellious against communist. I joined party to fight against communist occupation, because it was one finish occupation with German and start communist. Then when they liquidate, the government start to arrest many people. I have to leave this area. I came to Warsaw, still going to high school. The next year after, I met my husband, Zbigniew Michael Lorenc. And I get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Warsaw, and he-- he was a physician. He-- he had-- he finished medical school in 1939. He was older as I am. And we start to have family. I had four children. Now I have three. And we came here to America without permission.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Poland before you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since 1945 till 1967. Why? Because we start always to leave Poland. We knew that is no-- no good place. First, I was obsession about war all the time. And I knew that two giant are too close to us. I am afraid of war, this is first. My family had dream about America-- my grandparents also. And also, because my husband-- very, very dedicated surgeon-- because he was against communist as I was, we never get possibility to leave-- to leave Poland together to go for even for vacation. They wanted to keep one-- me or him. And also, they wanted-- they don't want to-- many people left Poland. They didn't want to leave-- to lose good physician. And also, they proposed-- proposed him very often, if you will be-- if you joined communist party, you will be professor in university. But he said, forget it. That's not for me. Her-- his family was very wealthy, and, of course, not communist. And I-- I-- I don't want to have nothing with-- with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a life did you have with him after the war in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not bad, because he was assistant of professor in the clinic, in medical school, in Warsaw. And also, he had a little private practice. It was not allowed to everybody, but he had a little. And you see, my-- my parents still helped me. I couldn't work, because I work with him in the office when he needed me. But to-- to four children, it was not-- not easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- what are the ages of your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now? 46-- my-- my oldest daughter passed away. She was born in 1949. She will-- she would be 48. But next daughter is from 50-- '51. She's 46. And my twins-- son-- are from '55. They are 42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My two sons are physician. One is in California-- twins, of course. One is in California-- dermatologist. And one is in New York-- a plastic surgeon. Both are married. I have one granddaughter from California, and I expect to have another baby from this son. My daughter has two children. My grandson, [INAUDIBLE] Madison, is in Carnegie Mellon University. I visited him there-- beautiful town. And my granddaughter is going to college this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does your daughter have an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She has together-- working together with my son-in-law. She-- she own one laboratory-- dental laboratory. And she's working with husband. He is dentist.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has the war affected the way you feel about [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] life today?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, after so bad experience, I can't still understand why is so many wars on the-- our Earth. Why? What for people want to have? That's enough-- what was-- what we passed-- but still is.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why so much hostility between people? This is not-- not a way to-- to live, with hostility. Love is only way. But what is my voice? It's nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to work for it-- to raise our children in love and understanding-- to-- to tolerance. This is our goal should be.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of message would you like to leave for your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Love. Understanding. This is important-- love to everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing this with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you introduce Irena to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's my older sister. She was with me all the-- all my life. And we were taken from Warsaw to concentration camp. And she passed everything what I passed in my life. She was very helpful. She saved my life during my very bad illness--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with my mother carrying me in small wagon to Poland. And she's always with me. Right now, we're living also very close to each other. But she-- she passed stroke. And she can't express so fluently, especially in English. But I know her character and her nature, that is very helpful, very idealistic, and very good person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irena, could you tell us what influenced you, having all of these experiences, that made an impact on your life today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think that maybe character of my parents do something. Probably in my mind must be the same as we-- by my parents. And I am sure that today, I can sleep. I can walk. I can do everything which I need. I did very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did everything for I need to do. And this thing, it's only to help other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it help to influence the way you raised your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I-- we-- we-- we-- both our parents, we do everything to help him. And today, my son is PhD in Harvard. And I am so glad to have son like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Would you like to introduce your family to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Here is sitting my sister, older sister, Irena Madjski. She passed with my experience during occupation time and being in a concentration camp. And still, we are very close until today. Next one is my daughter, my second daughter, Dorota Madison. She is working with husband. She is a-- a dental technician. She has own laboratory together with Dr. Madison, dentist. They are very close with me, each week coming to my house. Because he's working in the office in my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dorota, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: As you asked me before, how was my childhood-- I was a really sheltered from all the heartaches. And my childhood was normal, like any 11-years-old-- don't-- not like my mother's when she was 11-years-old and she was in a war. And so far, I'm happy. And I'm aware what happened. But-- it's terrible that it happened, but it-- it's in our past. And I hope it will never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're seeing here my parents, during wedding. This is Anna, maiden name, Szachowska, and my father, Pawel Wos. They were married about 1917, in Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to get this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? It was also found in ruins, in Warsaw, in Miodowa Street, where we were living. I found in bricks and dust. Therefore, this is a little, maybe, destroyed-- not so clear-- but my dearest parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again? You--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. On this picture is one of the first pictures, and also found in ruins, in Warsaw, in Miodowa Street. This is childhood of our family. On the left side is my sister, Irena Madjski, maiden name, Wos. The tallest boy is my oldest brother, [? Zenon ?] Paul Wos; and my other brother, George Odon Wos. I was sitting, because I was the youngest, with this ball, and the-- [LAUGHS] the smallest-- beautiful picture. In this picture is my aunt, my mother's sister, with me. I was very small-- maybe four-years-old on Krakowskie Street, in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: My aunt's name was [? Zafia ?] Szachowska. [INAUDIBLE] This is picture of me, of my house, when I was maybe six, seven years old. This is a room, sitting room, on nice couch, with many, many crochet, and some-- with my dog, named Lalka. And close to me was sitting two dolls-- my favorite. One was blonde, one was black-- porcelain dogs-- dolls, which I received from my aunt. This dog was very, very good-- very smart dog. On the wall are hanging picture of my brother from confirmation, and also is of my sister. Behind this couch, I remember I was standing beautiful-- beautiful-- not picture-- only the table-- very antique and beautiful. But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is picture of my mother with me. I was in age, 15. And my mother, I don't remember. But we took this picture in [? Atelier. ?] And I like it very much, because as previously-- previous pictures was also find in ruins in Warsaw. And I am very happy to keep them. This is picture of my wedding. I married my husband, Zbigniew Michael Lorenc. He was in this time, in army. He was physician. And I was very young-- 19-years-old girl-- very happy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is picture of my first, the oldest daughter, taken in 1950. Her name was Elizabeth Anna Lorenc. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is picture of my oldest daughter, Elizabeth Anna Lorenc, taken in her age 8 or 9. I don't remember, really. And it was done in Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is picture of my twin son, born in 1955, in January. They are very similar than-- this picture was done also in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Son-- [LAUGHS] son's names-- first, is on the left, Marek. And on the right is Zbigniew Paul Lorenc. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is picture of my children and me in my-- done in my house, in Glen Head, New York. On the left is standing my son, Zbigniew Paul Lorenc, who is physician in New York-- a plastic surgeon. My daughter, in the middle, Dorota Madison. And on the right is standing Marek Michael Lorenc, who is living in California. This picture was done 1953-- 1993-- sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here we see my medals I received. From the upper, from the left, is medal for being in concentration camp. Polish government gave to all people who were there. On the-- on down, on this medal is Polish army medal, which I received from England. On the right upper-- on the right upper is for being in underground army in Poland, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] AK. And this on-- under, is a medal from-- for participating in uprising in Warsaw as partisan. And this middle is only some-- not medal, but this is for special action in Poland-- action to fight with German people. And also small cross is for veterans who were were fighting during the second war.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Thank you very much for sharing all of this with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
